content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

August 1, 1996

Heather C. McLaughlin
Assistant City Attorney
City of Alameda
East Wing, Historical Alameda High School
Room 320
2250 Central Avenue
Alameda, California  94501

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-96-199a

Dear Ms. McLaughlin:

     This is in response to your request for follow-up advice re-
garding the ethics provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

QUESTION

     Are payments from the Pan-Pacific University to members of
the Alameda Reuse and Redevelopment Authority, for travel to
Korea, gifts to the agency or gifts to the individuals named in
the invitation?

CONCLUSION

     Under the facts you have provided, the payments will be
considered gifts to the individual public officials, subject to
the conflict-of-interest provisions in the Act.

FACTS

     The Alameda Reuse and Redevelopment Agency (the "ARRA") is a
joint powers authority formed under California law and created to
direct the reuse and redevelopment of the Naval Air Station (the
"Station") in Alameda. One of the proposed developers of the land
at the Station is Pan-Pacific University, which proposes to

___________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations
appear at Title 2, Sections 18000-18995 of the California Code of
Regulations.


428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814  (916) 322-5660

File No. A-96-199a
Page 2


develop a university at the Station. The University's title will
contain a reversion clause requiring the land to return to the
City of Alameda, if the land ceases to be used as a university.

In order to aid in financing the University, the University
is proposing to sponsor the ARRA Chair, the executive director,
and the planner on a trip to Korea to express the ARRA's support
of the project.

The invited team members identified in the invitation include
Ralph Appezzato, Mayor of Alameda, Kay Miller of the ARRA Board,
and Paul Tuttle of the ARRA Board.

ANALYSIS

In our earlier advice letter to you, we concluded that the
payments for travel to Korea would be considered gifts not subject
to the statutory gift limit. (See McLaughlin Advice Letter, No.
A-96-199.) In addition, we concluded that the gifts would be
subject to the conflict of interest provisions of the Act.

Under some circumstances, a gift is deemed to be provided to
a public official's agency, and not to the official. To determine
whether a gift has been made to an official's agency, and not to
the official who uses it, Regulation 18944.2(a) provides that the
following criteria must be satisfied:

(1) The agency receives and controls the pay-
ment.

(2) The payment is used for official agency
business.

(3) The agency, in its sole discretion,
determines the specific official or officials who
shall use the payment. However, the donor may
identify a specific purpose for the agency's use of
the payment, so long as the donor does not
designate the specific official or officials who
may use the payment.

(4) The agency memorializes the payment in a
written public record which embodies the require-
ments of subdivisions (a)(1) to (a)(3) of this
regulation set forth above and which:

(A) Identifies the donor and the of-
ficial, officials, or class of officials
receiving or using the payment;

(B) Describes the official agency use
and the nature and amount of the payment; and

File No. A-96-199a
Page 3


                    (C)  Is filed with the agency official
              who maintains the records of the agency's
              statements of economic interests where the
              agency has a specific office for the
              maintenance of such statements, or where no
              specific office exists for the maintenance of
              such statements, at a designated office of the
              agency, and the filing is done within 30 days
              of the receipt of the payment by the agency.

                    (Regulation 18944.2 [emphasis
                    added].)

       As we determined in our previous letter, these gifts do not
meet the requirements of this regulation. The donor of the gifts,
Pan-Pacific University, earmarked the gift to four specific of-
ficials, rather than to an agency. The "invited team members,"
according to the invitation, are the mayor of Alameda and two
board members of the ARRA. The University has designated not only
the purpose for the agencies' use of the payment, but also the
specific official or officials who may use the payment. This is
contrary to the provisions of Regulation 18944.2(a)(3).
Therefore, the gifts to an agency exception will not apply.

       You have asked for ways in which it would be possible to
structure the invitation in such a way as to avoid a conflict of
interest problem for the officials who will be going on the trip.
Regulation 18944.2 provides a limited exception to the general,
broad definition of a gift. The purpose of the regulation is to
provide a mechanism whereby a donor can provide goods or services
to a public agency without the incidental benefit to a public of-
ficial being a reportable gift. (Kaye Advice Letter, No. A-93-
490.) The regulation is not intended to provide a way to permit
gifts to specific officials to be characterized as gifts to the
officials' agency if that is not actually the case.

       As noted, the donor may not designate the specific official
or officials who may use the payment. (Regulation 18944.2(a)(3).)
Invitations and letters from the donor may be probative as to
whether or not the gift was earmarked; however, such invitations
will not necessarily be the determinative factor. In the past, we
have advised that even if a gift was not earmarked, other facts
may indicate that the gift was intended for specific officials.
(Kaye Advice Letter, supra.) In the Kaye letter, we opined that,
for instance, if the same high-level official and the official's
spouse took a trip for three consecutive years paid for by the
same donor, those facts would tend to show that the gift was ear-
marked for that official.2 Thus, the Kaye letter recognized that,

       2 The Kaye letter was based on the Stone opinion, the precursor
to Regulation 18944.2. The rule prohibiting earmarking the gift
for a specific official existed prior to the adoption of Regula-
tion 18944.2 and was central to the analysis in the Kaye letter.

File No. A-96-199a
Page 4


in some circumstances, the totality of the circumstances could
inform whether a gift was earmarked for a particular official or
officials.  This is just such a case.

The University issued the invitation to specific named of-
ficials. Upon discovering that the officials would be required to
report the gifts and would be subject to disqualification from
decisions involving the University, the University seeks to reissue the invitation and let the Board itself decide who will attend. There is no indication that the University's expections
about who will take the trip have changed at all from the initial
invitation. In fact, in your letter, you stated that you
anticipate that the Board will decide that the original invitees
will attend because "these individuals would all bring unique and
valuable knowledge with them." If the University reissues the
invitation and the same officials attend, that is strong evidence
that the gift is still earmarked and thus Regulation 18944.2 will
not apply.

Regulation 18941 provides that an official has received or
accepted a gift when the recipient "knows that he or she has
either actual possession of the gift or takes any action exercising direction or control over the gift." (Regulation 18941(b).)
Further, turning a gift over to another person does not negate the
acceptance of the gift. (Regulation 18941(b)(3).)

We have determined that in this case, Regulation 18944.2 will
not apply. Therefore, the gift of travel from the University will
have been donated to the individual officials, and they will be
deemed to have accepted the gift, if they take actual possession
of the gift, or exercise direction and control over the gift.
(Regulation 18941(b).) Accordingly, if the officials named in the
original invitation use the gift, or participate in redesignating
the gift to anyone else, they will be deemed to have accepted the
gift and must disqualify themselves from any decision that will
have a material financial effect on the donor of the gift. (Sections 87100; 87103.)

If the University chooses to rescind the earlier invitation
and donate the gift directly to the ARRA without restrictions, the
agency may accept the gift as a new gift subject to Regulation
18944.2 only if the officials who have already been earmarked in
the first invitation do not receive the gift and do not
participate in any decisions regarding who shall receive the gift.

This letter is limited to the unique facts present in this
case, where the donor has previously earmarked the gift to certain
officials. An interpretation permitting previously earmarked
gifts to be rescinded and then donated to the same officials would
essentially write the earmarking language out of Regulation
18944.2.

File No. A-96-199a
Page 5


     If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Liane Randolph
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:LR:ak

QUESTION: Are payments from the Pan-Pacific University to members of
the Alameda Reuse and Redevelopment Authority,
for travel to
Korea, gifts to the agency or gifts to the individuals named in
the invitation?

CONCLUSION: Under the facts you have provided, the payments will be
considered gifts to the individual public officials, subject to
the conflict-of-interest provisions in the Act.
